blocks:
Ayahs

سَأَلَ سَآئِلٌۢ بِعَذَابٍ وَاقِعٍ (١)
An asker asked for a punishment sure to befall (1)
لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَافِعٌ (٢)
—which none can avert from the faithless — (2)
مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ذِى ٱلْمَعَارِجِ (٣)
from Allah, Lord of the lofty stations. (3)

Content of Surah al-Ma'arij

It is well-known among commentators that Surah "al-Ma'arij" is a Meccan surah. According to the "Fihrist of Ibn al-Nadim," the book "Nazm al-Durar," and "Tanasuq al-Ayat wa al-Suwar"—as cited in "Tarikh al-Quran" by Abu Abd Allah Zanjani—this surah is the seventy-seventh surah revealed in Mecca.

However, this does not preclude the possibility that some of its verses were revealed in Medina. This is not unique to Surah "al-Ma'arij"; in many Meccan surahs of the Quran, there are verses revealed in Medina, and conversely, some Medinan surahs contain Meccan verses.

Allamah Amini has provided numerous examples of this in "al-Ghadir." ^1 The evidence that the opening verses of this surah were revealed in Medina lies in the many narrations that will be mentioned, God willing, in the commentary of these verses.

In any case, the characteristics of Meccan surahs—such as discussions regarding the principles of faith, especially the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and warnings to the polytheists and opponents—are fully evident in this surah. Overall, this surah consists of four sections:

The first section speaks of the swift punishment of someone who denied some of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) statements and said, "If this word is the truth, then let a punishment descend upon me," and it did descend (Verses 1 to 3). The second section mentions many characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, its precursors, and the states of the disbelievers on that day (Verses 4 to 18).

The third section of this surah describes certain qualities of good and bad people that lead them to either Paradise or Hell (Verses 19 to 34).

The fourth section includes warnings to the polytheists and deniers, returns once more to the issue of the Resurrection, and concludes the surah.

Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

"Whoever recites 'Sa'ala Sa'il' (Surah al-Ma'arij), Allah will grant him the reward of those who are observant of their trusts and their covenants, and those who are strictly observant of their prayers." ^1

Another hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) states:

"Whoever makes a habit of reciting 'Sa'ala Sa'il,' Allah will not question him on the Day of Resurrection regarding any sin he committed, and He will settle him in His Paradise with Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)." ^1

The same meaning has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

It is obvious that what makes a person eligible for such a great reward is a recitation accompanied by conviction and faith, followed by action—not merely reading the verses and surah without any reflection in one's soul, thought, or conduct.

^1: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 1, pp. 255-257. ^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 350. ^1: Ibid, p. 351.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِعٍ (1) لِلْكافِرينَ لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ (2) مِنَ اللَّهِ ذِي الْمَعارِجِ (3)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators and scholars of hadith have narrated an occasion of revelation for these verses, the summary of which is as follows:

"When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) appointed Ali (عليه السلام) as his successor on the day of 'Ghadir Khumm' and said regarding him: 'Whomsoever I am his Mawla (master/leader), then Ali is his Mawla,' it was not long before this news spread throughout the lands and cities. Nu'man ibn al-Harith al-Fihri came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: 'You commanded us to bear witness to the oneness of Allah and that you are His messenger, and we bore witness. Then you commanded us to Jihad, Hajj, fasting, prayer, and Zakat, and we accepted all of those. But you were not satisfied with this until you appointed this young man (referring to Ali عليه السلام) as your successor and said: "Whomsoever I am his Mawla, then Ali is his Mawla." Is this word from yourself or from Allah?' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: 'By Allah, besides whom there is no deity, this is from Allah.'

Nu'man turned away while saying: 'O Allah! If this word is the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky!' It was at this moment that a stone fell from the sky onto his head and killed him. Thereupon, the verse 'A petitioner asked for a punishment bound to happen, for the disbelievers; of it there is no preventer' was revealed."

What we have mentioned above is the substance of the account narrated in "Majma' al-Bayan" from Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani with his chain of transmission from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام). ^1 Many Sunni commentators and narrators of hadith have reported the same meaning with slight variations.

The late Allamah Amini in "al-Ghadir" cites it from thirty well-known Sunni scholars (mentioning the source and quoting the exact wording), including:

  • Tafsir Gharib al-Quran by Hafiz Abu Ubayd al-Harawi.
  • Tafsir Shifa' al-Sudur by Abu Bakr al-Naqqash al-Mawsili.
  • Tafsir al-Kashf wa al-Bayan by Abu Ishaq al-Tha'labi.
  • Tafsir of Abu Bakr Yahya al-Qurtubi.
  • Tadhkirah of Abu Ishaq al-Tha'labi.
  • Fara'id al-Simtayn by al-Hamwayni.
  • Durar al-Simtayn by Sheikh Muhammad al-Zarandi.
  • Tafsir al-Siraj al-Munir by Shams al-Din al-Shafi'i.
  • Sirah al-Halabiyyah.
  • Nur al-Absar by Sayyid Mu'min al-Shablanji.
  • Sharh al-Jami' al-Saghir of al-Suyuti by Shams al-Din al-Shafi'i.
  • And others. ^1

In many of these books, it is explicitly stated that the above verses were revealed in this regard. Of course, there is disagreement as to whether this person was Harith ibn al-Nu'man, Jabir ibn Nadhr, or Nu'man ibn al-Harith al-Fihri, and we know that this does not affect the core of the matter.

Naturally, some commentators or traditionists who accept the virtues of Ali (عليه السلام) with reluctance have raised various objections to this occasion of revelation, which we will address, God willing, at the end of the commentary discussion.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 352. ^1: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 1, pp. 239-246.

Commentary

Immediate Punishment

Surah al-Ma'arij begins by stating: "A petitioner asked for a punishment bound to happen" (سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِعٍ).

This petitioner, as mentioned in the occasion of revelation, was Nu'man ibn al-Harith or Nadhr ibn al-Harith, who became very angry when Ali (عليه السلام) was appointed to the position of Caliphate and Wilayah at Ghadir Khumm and the news spread through the cities. He came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked: "Is this from you or from Allah?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) explicitly said: "It is from Allah." He became even more upset and said: "O Allah! If this is the truth and from You, then send down a stone from the sky upon us." At that moment, a stone descended, struck his head, and killed him. ^1

In contrast to this interpretation, there is another more general one. According to this view, the meaning is that someone asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Upon whom will this punishment you speak of fall?" and the next verse answers: "It will be for the disbelievers."

According to a third interpretation, the petitioner is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, who requested punishment for the disbelievers, and it was sent down.

^1: The "ba" in "bi-adhabin waqi'" according to this interpretation is an extra "ba" for emphasis, and according to some, it means "an" (about), which aligns with the second interpretation. (Note that if "sa'ala" means "to request," it takes two objects; if it means "to inquire," its second object must be preceded by "an").

However, the first interpretation, besides being more consistent with the verse itself, precisely aligns with the numerous narrations regarding the occasion of revelation.

Then it adds: "This punishment is specifically for the disbelievers, and no one can prevent it" (لِلْكافِرينَ لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِعٌ). ^1

In the next verse, it points to the One from whom this punishment originates, saying: "This punishment is from Allah, the Lord of the ways of ascent (the heavens) where the angels ascend" (مِنَ اللَّهِ ذِي الْمَعارِجِ).

"Ma'arij" is the plural of "ma'raj," meaning a ladder or a place of ascent. Since Allah has established various ranks for the angels who advance toward proximity to Allah through a hierarchy, Allah is described as "Dhu al-Ma'arij" (Lord of the Ways of Ascent).

Indeed, it is these angels who are commissioned with the punishment of the disbelievers and criminals. It was they who descended upon Ibrahim al-Khalil (عليه السلام) and informed him that they were commissioned to destroy the people of Lut, and in the morning, they overturned the cities of those defiled and disgraceful people. [^2] They also carry out the commission of punishment regarding other criminals.

Some commentators have interpreted "Ma'arij" as "divine virtues and favors," and some as "angels," but the first meaning is more appropriate and more consistent with the linguistic concept of the word.

Key Point: The Vain Objections of Excuse-Seekers!

Usually, in cases where Quranic verses or narrations point to specific virtues regarding the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), some insist on ignoring the matter as much as possible, or giving it a distorted justification, pursuing the issue with a specific type of scrutiny and obsession—whereas if these virtues were for others, they would pass over them with tolerance and ease! A vivid example of this is the seven objections raised by Ibn Taymiyyah in the book "Minhaj al-Sunnah" regarding the hadiths mentioned about the occasion of revelation for the above verses, which we will briefly address:

  1. The story of Ghadir took place after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) return from the Farewell Pilgrimage, i.e., in the tenth year of the Hijra, while Surah al-Ma'arij is among the Meccan surahs revealed before the Hijra.

Answer: As we mentioned earlier, many surahs are called Meccan even though some of their initial verses were revealed in Medina according to the explicit statements of commentators; conversely, there are surahs officially called Medinan, but some of their verses were revealed in Mecca.

  1. In this hadith, it is stated that "Harith ibn al-Nu'man" met the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in "Abtah," and we know that "Abtah" is a valley in "Mecca," which is inconsistent with the revelation of the verse after the story of Ghadir.

Answer: Firstly, the expression "Abtah" is only in some narrations, not all. Secondly, "Abtah" and "Batha" refer to any gravelly land where a flood flows, and coincidentally, there are areas in the land of Medina called "Abtah" or "Batha," which are frequently mentioned in Arabic poetry and narrations.

  1. The verse "And when they said: O Allah! If this is the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky" (al-Anfal: 32) was certainly revealed after the Battle of Badr, years before the story of Ghadir.

Answer: No one has said that the occasion of revelation for that verse is the story of Ghadir; rather, the discussion is about the verse "A petitioner asked for a punishment bound to happen." As for Verse 32 of Surah al-Anfal, it is something that "Harith ibn al-Nu'man" used in his own speech, and this has nothing to do with the occasion of revelation. However, extreme prejudices cause one to be oblivious to such a clear point.

  1. The Holy Quran says: "But Allah would not punish them while you are among them, nor would Allah punish them while they seek forgiveness" (al-Anfal: 33). This verse says that in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), no punishment will ever descend.

Answer: What is acceptable is that a general and collective punishment did not exist because of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but private and individual punishments repeatedly fell upon individuals. Islamic history bears witness that several individuals like "Abu Zam'ah," "Malik ibn Talalah," "Hakam ibn Abi al-As," and others were afflicted with punishment due to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) curse or otherwise. Furthermore, the above verse has other interpretations according to which arguing with it here is not possible (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 7, p. 154, under Verse 33 of al-Anfal).

  1. If such an occasion of revelation were correct, it should have become as famous as the story of the "People of the Elephant" (Ashab al-Fil).

Answer: This occasion of revelation is sufficiently famous and well-known, and we mentioned above that it appears in at least thirty books of commentary and hadith. Moreover, it is strange to expect an individual incident to be reflected in the same way as a general incident like the story of the People of the Elephant; for that story had a universal aspect, encompassing all of Mecca, and a large army was destroyed during it, whereas the story of "Harith ibn al-Nu'man" concerns only one person!

  1. From this hadith, it is inferred that "Harith ibn al-Nu'man" accepted the foundations of Islam; how is it possible for a Muslim in the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to be afflicted with such a punishment?

Answer: This objection also stems from intense prejudice, for the above hadiths clearly show that he not only rejected the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) statement but also protested against Allah as to why He gave such a command regarding Ali (عليه السلام) to His Prophet! This is considered the most severe level of disbelief and apostasy.

  1. In famous books like "al-Isti'ab" where the names of the "Companions" (Sahaba) are listed, we do not see the name of "Harith ibn al-Nu'man."

Answer: What is mentioned in this book and its likes regarding the names of the Sahaba concerns only a portion of them. For example, in the book "Usd al-Ghabah," which is one of the most important books enumerating the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions, only 7,554 people are listed, while we know that in the Farewell Pilgrimage alone, one hundred thousand people or more were in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Therefore, there is no doubt that many of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions are not named in these books. ^1

[^2]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 11, pp. 98 and 106. ^1: For more details regarding the above answers and historical or narrative evidence for each, please study the precious book "al-Ghadir," Vol. 1, pp. 247-266.

Ayahs

تَعْرُجُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِى يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُۥ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ (٤)
The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose span is fifty thousand years. (4)
فَٱصْبِرْ صَبْرًا جَمِيلًا (٥)
So be patient, with a patience that is graceful. (5)
إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُۥ بَعِيدًا (٦)
Indeed they see it to be far off, (6)
وَنَرَىٰهُ قَرِيبًا (٧)
and We see it to be near. (7)

Commentary

A Day the Length of Fifty Thousand Years!

After mentioning the story of the worldly punishment of the one who had requested divine chastisement, the discourse enters the discussion of the Resurrection and the afterlife punishments of the criminals on that Day. First, It says:

"The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him (Allah) in a Day the measure of which is fifty thousand years" (تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ).

Certainly, the "ascent of the angels" does not refer to a physical ascent, but rather a spiritual one; meaning they hasten toward the station of proximity to Allah. On that Day, which is the Day of Resurrection, they are ready to receive His command and execute it, just as we mentioned in the commentary of verse 17 of Surah al-Haqqah regarding the phrase "And the angels will be on its edges" (وَالْمَلَكُ عَلى‌ أَرْجائِها), meaning that on that Day, they stand around the heavens, prepared to carry out any command. [^1] The "Spirit" (al-Ruh) refers to "al-Ruh al-Amin" (the Trustworthy Spirit), the greatest of the angels, who is also mentioned in Surah al-Qadr: "The angels and the Spirit descend therein by permission of their Lord for every matter" (تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ) (Qadr: 4).

Of course, "Spirit" has various meanings, and in each instance, it may convey a specific concept depending on the available context: the human soul, the Spirit in the sense of the Quran, the Spirit in the sense of the Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus), and in the sense of the Angel of Revelation—all are meanings of "Spirit" referred to in other verses of the Quran. [^2] As for the expression "fifty thousand years," it is from the perspective that that Day will last this long according to the years of this world. This does not contradict what is mentioned in verse 5 of Surah al-Sajdah, which determines its measure as one thousand years, because as stated in the narrations, there are fifty stations in the Resurrection, and each station lasts for one thousand years. [^3] Some have also suggested that the number "fifty thousand years" is for "multiplicity" (emphasis on greatness) rather than a literal count, meaning that Day is extremely long.

In any case, this pertains to the criminals, the oppressors, and the disbelievers. Therefore, it is mentioned in a hadith from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that after this verse was revealed, someone asked: "O Messenger of Allah! How long that Day is?" He replied:

"By the One in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad, it will be lightened for the believer until it becomes lighter for him than a single obligatory prayer he performs in this world." [^4]

In the next verse, addressing the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, It says: "So be patient with a beautiful patience, and be steadfast against their mockery, denial, and harassment" (فَاصْبِرْ صَبْراً جَمِيلًا). "Beautiful patience" (Sabr Jamil) means "a patience that is beautiful and noteworthy"; it is that patience and perseverance which is continuous, where despair and hopelessness find no way, and which is not accompanied by restlessness, agitation, complaining, or moaning; otherwise, it is not "beautiful." [^5]

Then It adds: "Indeed, they see it as distant" (إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيداً).

"But We see it as near" (وَ نَراهُ قَرِيباً). They do not believe at all that such a Day exists where the account of all creatures will be settled and their smallest words and deeds will be calculated—and that too on a Day that lasts fifty thousand years. But in reality, they have not recognized Allah and they harbor doubt and hesitation regarding His power.

They say: "Is it possible for decayed bones and dust, every particle of which has been scattered in some corner, to be gathered once again and clothed in the garment of life?" (As the Quran has quoted these expressions from them in other verses). Furthermore, is a day of fifty thousand years even possible?!

Interestingly, modern science states that the length of a day on every celestial body in the heavens differs from other bodies, as it depends on the amount of time that body takes to rotate once around its axis. Therefore, a day on the Moon is as long as two weeks on Earth. It is even said that with the passage of time, the Earth may decrease its rotational speed, and the length of one day could become equivalent to a month, a year, or hundreds of years.

We do not say the Day of Resurrection is exactly like this, but we say that a day equivalent to fifty thousand years is not something strange, even by the standards of the physical world.

[^1]: Other interpretations have been mentioned for the "ascent of the angels," none of which seem appropriate, including: the meaning is the time of the descent and ascent of the angels from the beginning of the world to its end, which totals 50,000 years, being the lifespan of the entire world. However, the subsequent verses clearly show that the discussion is about the Day of Resurrection, not this world (take note). [^2]: Refer to Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 250 to 253. [^3]: This hadith is narrated in the Amali of the Sheikh from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام (according to the narration in Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 5, p. 413). [^4]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 10, p. 353 and Qurtubi, vol. 10, p. 6761. [^5]: Regarding "Beautiful Patience," we have provided more explanation in Tafsir Nemouneh (vol. 9, p. 351) in the story of Ya'qub and Yusuf.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ تَكُونُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ كَٱلْمُهْلِ (٨)
The day when the sky will be like molten copper, (8)
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ (٩)
and the mountains like [tufts of] dyed wool, (9)
وَلَا يَسْـَٔلُ حَمِيمٌ حَمِيمًا (١٠)
and no friend will inquire about [the welfare of his] friend, (10)
يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ ۚ يَوَدُّ ٱلْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِى مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِئِذٍۭ بِبَنِيهِ (١١)
[though] they will be placed within each other’s sight. The guilty one will wish he could ransom himself from the punishment of that day at the price of his children, (11)
وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَأَخِيهِ (١٢)
his spouse and his brother, (12)
وَفَصِيلَتِهِ ٱلَّتِى تُـْٔوِيهِ (١٣)
his kin, which had sheltered him (13)
وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يُنجِيهِ (١٤)
and all those who are upon the earth, if that might deliver him. (14)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهَا لَظَىٰ (١٥)
Never! Indeed, it is a blazing fire, (15)
نَزَّاعَةً لِّلشَّوَىٰ (١٦)
which strips away the scalp. (16)
تَدْعُوا۟ مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (١٧)
It invites him, who has turned back [from the truth] and forsaken [it], (17)
وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعَىٰٓ (١٨)
amassing [wealth] and hoarding [it]. (18)

Commentary

The Day When No Intimate Friend Will Ask After His Friend

In these verses, the previous discussions regarding the Resurrection continue with more elaboration and explanation. It says:

"The Day that the sky will be like molten metal," (يَوْمَ تَكُونُ السَّماءُ كَالْمُهْلِ‌) [^1]

"And the mountains will be like tufts of wool," (وَ تَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ‌).

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "Yawm" (Day), several possibilities have been suggested, but the best among them is that it is a substitute (badal) for "qariban" (near) in the previous verse, or it relates to an omitted verb such as "udhkur" (remember).

"Muhl" (rhyming with qufl) means molten metal, and sometimes it refers to the dregs that settle in olive oil; here, the first meaning is more appropriate, though in the context of a simile, there is little difference between them.

"Ihn" means carded wool that has been dyed.

Indeed, on that day, the heavens will disintegrate and melt, and the mountains will be crushed and pulverized, then scattered in the atmosphere by a violent wind, like wool carried away by a gale. Since mountains have various colors, they are likened to dyed wool. After this destruction, a new world will be created, and human beings will begin their new life.

When the Resurrection is established in that new world, the state of accounting and the investigation of deeds will be so terrifying that everyone will be preoccupied with themselves; no one will attend to another, and "no intimate friend will ask after his friend" (وَ لا يَسْئَلُ حَمِيمٌ حَمِيماً). Everyone is busy with themselves, and each person is thinking of their own salvation, just as stated in verse 37 of Surah Abasa: "For every man, that Day, will be a matter adequate for him" (لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ‌). [^2]

[^1]: "Hamim," as we have previously noted, originally means hot and scalding water; subsequently, it has been applied to warm and very intimate friends.

[^2]: Other interpretations have been mentioned for the above verse, including that no one asks about another's condition because everyone's state is clear from their faces—and where things are evident, what need is there for explanation? Another is that no one asks another to take responsibility for their deeds because it is impossible. However, the first interpretation is the correct one.

It is not that intimate friends will not recognize each other there; rather, they will specifically "be made to see each other," yet each person is preoccupied with their own affair (يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ‌). [^1] The issue is that the horror and terror are too great for one to think of another.

Continuing this discourse and depicting that terrifying scene, it adds: The situation is such that "the criminal would wish that he could ransom himself from the punishment of that Day by his children" (يَوَدُّ الْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِي مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمِئِذٍ بِبَنِيهِ‌).

Not only his children, but he wishes to give "his wife and his brother" (وَ صاحِبَتِهِ وَ أَخِيهِ‌).

"And his nearest kindred who sheltered him" (وَ فَصِيلَتِهِ الَّتِي تُؤْوِيهِ‌).

"And whoever is on earth entirely [so] then it might save him" (وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً ثُمَّ يُنْجِيهِ‌).

[^1]: Although "Hamim" is singular in both instances, the two pronouns in "yubassarunahum" are plural because it carries a generic meaning.

Indeed, Allah's punishment on that day is so horrific that man wishes to sacrifice his dearest loved ones—summarized here in four groups (children, spouses, brothers, and close kin who were his helpers)—all to save himself. Not only them, but he is ready for all the people on earth to be sacrificed for his release from the punishment! "Yawaddu" is from the root "wudd" (rhyming with hubb), meaning "to love" accompanied by "longing," and according to Raghib, it is used in either of these two meanings (or rather, in both).

"Yaftadi" is from the root "fida’," meaning to protect oneself from calamities and difficulties by paying something.

"Fasilah" means the "clan," kindred, or tribe from which a person has branched out (infasala).

"Tu’wihi" is from the root "iwa’," meaning to join one thing to another; subsequently, it has been used to mean providing shelter.

A group of commentators have said that the use of "thumma" (then) in the phrase "thumma yunjih" indicates that they know these ransoms are unlikely to work (as "thumma" usually denotes a sequence with a delay or distance).

But in response to all these longings and desires, He says: "By no means!" No ransom or sacrifice will be accepted (كلا). "Indeed, it is a Flame [of Hell]" (إِنَّها لَظى‌).

It constantly blazes, burning everything it finds in its vicinity and path.

"Stripping away the limbs and the scalp" (نَزَّاعَةً لِلشَّوى‌). "Ladha" means a pure flame of fire, and it is also one of the names of Hell; in the above verses, both meanings are possible.

"Nazza’ah" means something that repeatedly tears away or strips off.

And "shawa" means the hands, feet, and extremities of the body; sometimes it has also come to mean roasting, but here the first meaning is intended. For when a blazing and flaming fire reaches something, it first burns and separates its extremities, sides, and branches.

Some commentators have interpreted "shawa" here as the skin of the body, some as the scalp, and some as the flesh of the shins; all of these are included in the broad concept we mentioned. And the wonder is that despite all this agony, there is no death involved!

Then He refers to those who are the prey of such a fire, saying: "It calls him who turned his back and fled [from truth]" (تَدْعُوا مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَ تَوَلَّى‌).

"And collected [wealth] and hoarded [it]" (وَ جَمَعَ فَأَوْعى‌). In this way, this blazing fire—either through its state and a specific attraction it has toward criminals, or through the literal speech that Allah has granted it—constantly calls them and summons them to itself. Those who possess these two attributes: "turned their backs on faith and turned away from the obedience of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله" and on the other hand "were constantly thinking of collecting wealth from both forbidden and lawful sources and hoarding it without paying attention to the rights of the needy, or without knowing the philosophy of this divine blessing, which is wealth."

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا (١٩)
Indeed man has been created covetous: (19)
إِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ جَزُوعًا (٢٠)
anxious when an ill befalls him (20)
وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلْخَيْرُ مَنُوعًا (٢١)
and grudging [charity] when good comes his way (21)
إِلَّا ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٢٢)
—[all are such] except the prayerful, (22)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ دَآئِمُونَ (٢٣)
those who persevere in their prayers (23)
وَٱلَّذِينَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَّعْلُومٌ (٢٤)
and there is a known share in whose wealth (24)
لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (٢٥)
for the beggar and the deprived, (25)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٢٦)
and who affirm the Day of Retribution, (26)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ (٢٧)
and those who are apprehensive of the punishment of their Lord (27)
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ (٢٨)
(there is indeed no security from the punishment of their Lord) (28)

Commentary

Attributes of Worthy Human Beings

After mentioning a portion of the punishments of the Resurrection, the text proceeds to describe the attributes of the faithless and, in contrast, the true believers, so it may become clear why one group deserves punishment and the other deserves salvation.

First, It says: "Indeed, man was created anxious and impatient" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً).

"When evil touches him, he is full of lamentation and impatience" (إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً).

"And when good touches him, he withholds it from others" (وَ إِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً).

A group of commentators and lexicographers have interpreted halū‘ (هلوع) to mean "greedy," while others have interpreted it as "impatient." According to the first interpretation, three negative moral traits in such humans are pointed out here: "greed," "lamentation," and "stinginess." According to the second interpretation, two points are mentioned: "lamentation" and "stinginess," because the second and third verses serve as an explanation for the meaning of halū‘.

It is also possible that both meanings are combined in this word, as these two attributes are inseparable; greedy people are often stingy and have little endurance in the face of adverse events, and the reverse is also true.

Here, a question or questions arise: If Allah created man for happiness and perfection, how did He place evil and badness in his nature? Furthermore, is it possible for Allah to create something with a certain attribute and then condemn His own creation?

Beyond all this, the Quran explicitly states in Surah At-Tin, verse 4:

لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ: "We have certainly created man in the best of forms."

Certainly, the meaning is not that man's appearance is good while his inner self is ugly and bad; rather, the entire creation of man is in the "best of forms." Likewise, other verses praise the high status of man. How are these verses compatible with the verse under discussion?

The answer to all these questions becomes clear by noting one point: Allah has created powers, instincts, and attributes in man that are potentially the means for his evolution, progress, and happiness. Therefore, the aforementioned attributes and instincts are not inherently bad; rather, they are tools for perfection. However, when these same attributes are placed on a deviant path and are misused, they become the source of misery, misfortune, evil, and corruption.

For example, this very "greed" (حرص) is a force that does not allow man to quickly cease his efforts and struggles or to be satisfied upon reaching a blessing. This is a burning thirst that dominates man's existence. If this attribute is used in the path of acquiring knowledge, and man is "greedy" for knowledge—or in other words, a restless lover of science—it is certainly a means for his perfection. But if it is used in the path of material things, it becomes a source of evil, misery, and stinginess.

In other words: this attribute is a branch of self-love, and we know that self-love is something that drives man toward perfection. However, if it is placed on a deviant path, it moves toward monopolization, stinginess, envy, and the like.

The same applies to other blessings. Allah has created a great power within the heart of the atom, which is certainly useful and beneficial. But whenever this internal power of the atom is misused to build destructive bombs instead of power plants and other industrial tools, it becomes a source of evil and corruption.

With this explanation, one can reconcile all the verses in the Holy Quran regarding man. [^1]

[^1]: We have provided another explanation under the title "Man in the Holy Quran" in Volume 8 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 239 to 241 (under Jonah, verse 12).

Then, it proceeds to mention the attributes of worthy human beings as an exception, stating nine prominent attributes, saying: "Except those who pray" (إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ).

"Those who are constant in their prayer" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى‌ صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ). This is their first characteristic: they have a continuous connection with the threshold of the Almighty Lord, and this connection is maintained through prayer—the prayer that restrains man from indecency and wrongdoing, the prayer that nurtures man's soul and spirit and keeps him constantly in remembrance of Allah. This continuous attention prevents negligence, pride, drowning in the sea of lusts, and being enslaved by Satan and carnal desires.

Obviously, the meaning of constancy in prayer is not that they are always in the state of performing the ritual prayer, but rather that they perform the prayer at its appointed times.

Fundamentally, any good deed has a positive effect on man only when it is continuous. Therefore, we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"The most beloved of deeds to Allah is that which is continuous, even if it is little." [^1] It is noteworthy that we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "The meaning is that whenever a person takes upon himself any of the supererogatory prayers (nawafil), he should always continue them." [^2]

[^1]: Al-Mu'jam al-Mufahras li-Alfaz al-Hadith, Vol. 2, p. 160 (root: d-w-m).

[^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 415.

And in another hadith narrated from the same Imam عليه السلام, he said: "This verse refers to the supererogatory prayer (nafilah), and the verse 'And those who guard their prayer' (which comes a few verses later) refers to the obligatory prayer." [^1] This distinction might be because the expression "guarding" (muhafazat) is suitable for obligatory prayers, which must be performed precisely at specific times, while the expression "constancy" (dawam) is suitable for recommended prayers, as a person may perform them or sometimes omit them.

In any case, after mentioning prayer—which is the best of deeds and the best state of the believers—He proceeds to their second characteristic, adding: "And those in whose wealth there is a known right" (وَ الَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ).

"For the petitioner and the deprived" (لِلسَّائِلِ وَ الْمَحْرُومِ). In this way, they maintain both their connection with the Creator and their bond with Allah's creation.

Some commentators believe that the "known right" here refers to "Zakat," which has a specific amount, and its recipients include the "petitioner" and the "deprived." However, we know that this Surah is Meccan, and the ruling of Zakat was not revealed in Mecca, or if it was, it did not have a fixed amount. Therefore, others believe that the "known right" refers to something other than Zakat that a person considers necessary upon himself to give to the needy. This interpretation is supported by a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse:

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 416.

Is this something other than Zakat? He replied:

"Yes, this is about a man whom Allah grants wealth and property, so he takes out from it a thousand, two thousand, three thousand, or more or less, and with it, he maintains family ties (silat al-rahim) and removes the hardship of his people." [^1] The difference between the "petitioner" (sa'il) and the "deprived" (mahrum) is that the "petitioner" is one who voices his need and asks, while the "deprived" is one whose modesty and shame prevent him from asking. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "The deprived is one who toils in business and work, but his life has become complicated [financially]." [^2] This hadith also fits the aforementioned interpretation, as such individuals are less likely to ask.

A more detailed explanation regarding the said right and the interpretation of "petitioner" and "deprived" is found in Volume 22 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 323 (under Adh-Dhariyat, verse 19).

In any case, performing this act has a social effect on one hand, as it fights poverty and deprivation, and on the other hand, it leaves a moral effect on those who practice it, washing their hearts and souls from greed, stinginess, and worldliness.

The next verse points to their third characteristic, adding: "And those who believe in the Day of Recompense" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ).

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 417 (Hadiths 25 and 27).

And in the fourth characteristic, it says: "And those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ).

"For indeed, the punishment of their Lord is not something to feel secure from" (إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ). On one hand, they have faith in the Day of Recompense, and considering the expression yusaddiqun (يصدقون), which is a present-tense verb indicating continuity, they are constantly mindful that there is an accounting, a book, and a recompense.

Some commentators have interpreted it as "practical confirmation," meaning performing duties and abandoning prohibitions, but the literal sense of the verse is absolute, encompassing both intellectual and practical confirmation.

However, since it is possible for someone to believe in the Day of Recompense but not consider himself subject to punishment, it says: "they never feel secure," meaning they constantly feel a sense of responsibility, considering their good deeds as small and their sins as large. Thus, in a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, it is narrated that he advised his son, saying: "O my son!

Fear Allah with such a fear that if you were to bring the good deeds of all the people of the earth, you would think He might not accept them from you! And hope in Allah with such a hope that if you had the sins of all the people of the earth, you would think He might forgive you!" [^1]

[^1]: Jami' al-Akhbar, p. 113.

Even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself used to say:

"No one shall enter Paradise by his deeds alone." They asked, "Not even you, O Messenger of Allah?" He replied, "Not even I, unless Allah envelops me in His mercy!"

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ (٢٩)
and those who guard their private parts (29)
إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ (٣٠)
(apart from their spouses and their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (30)
فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ (٣١)
but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors) (31)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ (٣٢)
and those who keep their trusts and covenants, (32)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِشَهَـٰدَٰتِهِمْ قَآئِمُونَ (٣٣)
and those who are observant of their testimonies, (33)
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٣٤)
and those who are watchful of their prayers. (34)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ مُّكْرَمُونَ (٣٥)
They will be in gardens, held in honour. (35)

Commentary

Another Part of the Characteristics of the People of Paradise

In the preceding verses, four characteristics of the true believers and those who will dwell in Paradise on the Day of Resurrection were mentioned. These verses point to five additional characteristics, making a total of nine attributes.

In the first description, He says: "They are those who guard their private parts [^1] from unchastity" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ‌).

"Except with regard to their spouses or those whom their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy" (إِلَّا عَلى‌ أَزْواجِهِمْ أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ‌). Undoubtedly, the sexual instinct is one of the rebellious instincts of man and the source of many sins, to the extent that some believe a trace of this instinct can be found in every major criminal case. Therefore, controlling and maintaining its boundaries is one of the significant signs of piety (taqwa). For this reason, the control of this instinct is mentioned after the discussion of prayer, helping the needy, faith in the Day of Resurrection, and fear of Divine punishment.

The exception mentioned thereafter shows that the logic of Islam is never to completely eradicate or destroy this instinct, nor for one to act against the law of creation like monks and priests—an act that is often impossible, and even if possible,

[^1]: "Furuj" is the plural of "farj," an allusion to the reproductive organs.

is illogical. Consequently, even monks have been unable to remove this instinct from the scene of life; while they do not officially marry, many of them "do that other thing" when they are in private! The scandals that have arisen from this path are not few; Christian historians such as Will Durant and others have pulled back the curtain on this matter.

The term "spouses" (azwaj) includes both permanent and temporary wives. The fact that some have imagined this verse negates "temporary marriage" is because they do not realize that it, too, is a form of marriage.

In the following verse, to place further emphasis on this subject, it adds: "But whoever seeks beyond that, then those are the transgressors who exceed the Divine limits" (فَمَنِ ابْتَغى‌ وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ‌). In this way, Islam lays out a plan for a society where natural instincts are answered, yet it remains untainted by indecency, sexual corruption, and the resulting vices.

Of course, from the perspective of Islam, bondwomen possessed many of the conditions and legal disciplines of a spouse, although in our current age and time, the subject matter itself is defunct.

Then, pointing to their second and third characteristics, it says: "And those who are keepers of their trusts and their covenant" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَ عَهْدِهِمْ راعُونَ‌). Certainly, "trust" (amanat) has a broad meaning that encompasses not only the material trusts of people of every kind but also includes the Divine trusts and those of the Prophets and the Infallible Leaders (peace be upon them).

Every single Divine blessing is a trust among His trusts. Social positions, and especially the station of governance, are among the most important trusts. Hence, in the famous Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse "Indeed, Allah commands you to render trusts to their owners" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تُؤَدُّوا الْأَماناتِ إِلى‌ أَهْلِها), it is explicitly stated: "Hand over the Wilayah (authority) and governance to its rightful people." [^1] In verse 72 of Surah Al-Ahzab, we also read that the matter of duty and responsibility is introduced as a great Divine trust: "Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth..." (إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمانَةَ عَلَى السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). Most importantly, the religion and creed of God and His Book, the Quran, are His great trust, for which one must strive to preserve.

"Covenant" ('ahd) also has a broad concept that includes both human covenants and Divine covenants and pledges.

This is because a "covenant" is any form of commitment or obligation that a person gives to another, and undoubtedly, one who believes in Allah and His Prophet has accepted extensive commitments through this faith.

In Islam, extraordinary importance is given to keeping trusts and being bound to commitments, and it is introduced as one of the most important signs of faith.

We had a more detailed discussion on this subject in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 432, under verse 58 of Surah An-Nisa.

And in the fourth characteristic, it adds: "And those who stand firm in their testimonies" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ بِشَهاداتِهِمْ قائِمُونَ‌).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 380.

This is because the establishment of just testimony and the refusal to conceal it is one of the most important foundations for establishing justice in human society.

Those who say, "Why should we buy the enmity of this person or that person by testifying against them and create trouble for ourselves?" are individuals indifferent to human rights, lacking a social spirit, and uncommitted to the implementation of justice.

Therefore, the Quran, in numerous verses, has invited Muslims to give truthful testimony and has considered its concealment a sin. [^1] In Islamic jurisprudence, testimony also holds a special importance and is considered the basis for proving many individual and social rights, having its own specific rulings.

In the final characteristic, which is actually the ninth description in this set, it returns once again to the matter of prayer, just as it began with prayer. It says: "And those who guard their prayer" (وَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى‌ صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ‌).

As we pointed out earlier, based on certain indications, it seems that "prayer" here refers to the "obligatory prayer," while in the previous verse, it referred to the "supererogatory (nafilah) prayer."

Furthermore, the first characteristic referred to the "continuity" of prayer, but here the talk is about guarding its etiquettes, conditions, pillars, and specifics—etiquettes that preserve the outward form of prayer from what causes invalidity, strengthen the soul of the prayer which is the presence of the heart, and eliminate the moral obstacles that block its acceptance. Therefore, it is by no means considered a repetition.

[^1]: "Al-Baqarah: 283 and 140," "Al-Ma'idah: 106," and "At-Talaq: 2."

This beginning and ending show that among all these characteristics, attention to prayer is the most superior and important of them. Why should it not be so, when "prayer is a sublime school of training" and "the most important means for the refinement of souls and the purification of society"?

At the conclusion of this discourse, it describes the final destination of those possessing these attributes, just as it explained the final destination of the criminals in the preceding verses. Here, in one short and meaningful sentence, it says: "Those will be in gardens, honored" (أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ‌). [^1] Why should they not be honored? For they are the guests of Allah, and the Powerful and Merciful Lord has provided all means of hospitality for them. In reality, these two expressions ("Gardens" and "Honored") refer to the material and spiritual blessings that encompass this group.

[^1]: "Fi jannatin" is the predicate (khabar) for "ula'ika," and "mukramun" is the second predicate; or "mukramun" is the predicate and "fi jannatin" is related to it (take note).

Ayahs

فَمَالِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ (٣٦)
What is the matter with the faithless that they scramble toward you, (36)
عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ عِزِينَ (٣٧)
from left and right, in groups? (37)
أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ (٣٨)
Does each man among them hope to enter the garden of bliss? (38)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
Never! Indeed, We created them from what they know. (39)
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ وَٱلْمَغَـٰرِبِ إِنَّا لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٤٠)
So I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests that We are able (40)
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٤١)
to replace them with [others] better than them and We are not to be outmaneuvered. (41)

Commentary

With What Honor Do You Covet Paradise?!

In the preceding verses of this Surah, there were discussions regarding the signs of the believers and the disbelievers and the fate of each of these two groups. In the above verses, the discourse returns once again to a description of the state of the disbelievers and their mockery of sacred matters.

Some have stated that these verses were revealed concerning groups of polytheists who, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the verses of the Resurrection to the Muslims in Mecca, would come from every corner and say: "If there is indeed a Resurrection, our condition in that world will be better than these people who have believed in you, just as our condition in this world is better than theirs!" In response to them, the Quran says:

"What is the matter with those who disbelieve, that they hasten before you," (فَما لِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ).

"Coming from the right and from the left, in groups," and coveting Paradise (عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَ عَنِ الشِّمالِ عِزِينَ).

"Does every one of them covet to be admitted into a Garden of Pleasure?" (أَ يَطْمَعُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ). With what faith and with what deed do they claim such worthiness for themselves?! "Muhti'in" is the plural of "muhti'", meaning one who stretches out his neck and walks swiftly in search of something, and sometimes it simply means stretching the neck to receive news.

"Izin" is the plural of "izah" (on the pattern of hibah), meaning scattered groups. Its original root is "azw" (on the pattern of jadhw), meaning to attribute or relate. Since every group that stays together has a relation and connection to one another, or pursues a single goal, a group or assembly is called "izah."

In any case, the self-centered and egoistic polytheists had many such baseless claims. They imagined that the prosperity of their material life—which was often obtained through illegitimate means, plunder, and the like—was a proof of their high status before Allah and their being beloved by the Lord. Then, through a meaningless comparison, they claimed high ranks for themselves in the Resurrection.

It is true that they did not believe in the "Resurrection" in the form described by the Quran; however, they would sometimes discuss it as a possibility, saying that if there were a Resurrection, we would be such-and-all in the other world. Perhaps they even said this out of mockery.

Here, the Holy Quran proceeds to answer them, saying: "By no means [shall they enter]! Indeed, We have created them from what they know" (كَلَّا إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ). In reality, Allah intends with this sentence, firstly, to break their pride, for He says: You yourselves know better from what We created you?

From an insignificant drop of semen, from a foul and lowly fluid; so what is all this pride and arrogance for?

Secondly, it provides an answer to those who mock the Resurrection: if you have doubt in the matter of the Resurrection, go and examine the state of the sperm-drop, and see how We create a wondrous being from an insignificant drop of water, which takes on a new creation every day during the embryonic developments.

Is the Creator of man from a sperm-drop not capable of clothing man in the garment of life after he has turned to dust?

Thirdly, how can these people covet Paradise while they have so many sinful deeds in their record of actions? For a being created from an insignificant sperm-drop possesses no nobility from a material perspective. If there is any nobility, it is due to faith and righteous deeds, which they lack. So how do they expect to step into the gardens of Paradise? [^1]

Then, to emphasize this point, He adds: "So I swear by the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets that We are surely able..." (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَ الْمَغارِبِ إِنَّا لَقادِرُونَ).

"To replace them with [others] better than them; and We are not to be outdone" (عَلى‌ أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْراً مِنْهُمْ وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ). This sentence may refer to the fact that not only are We able to return them to life and a new existence after they have turned to dust, but We can also transform them into more perfect and better beings, and nothing shall hinder this work.

[^1]: Other possibilities have also been suggested in the interpretation of the above verse, including that the phrase "مِمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ" (from what they know) means that We created them with intellect and consciousness, not like animals and beasts; therefore, they are responsible for their actions. Another is that it means We created them for purposes they know—namely, duty and obedience. However, these possibilities seem very remote; thus, most commentators have accepted the meaning mentioned above.

And in this way, the above expression is a continuation of the discussion on the Resurrection.

Or it may be an indication that there is no obstacle for Us to destroy you as a punishment for your deeds and replace you with worthy, aware, and believing individuals to be the helpers and companions of Our Prophet, and nothing will prevent Us from this.

Therefore, if We urge you to believe, it is not out of need or inability, but rather the general interest of the education and guidance of humanity necessitates it.

The expression "بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَ الْمَغارِبِ" (the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets) may refer to the fact that the same God who is capable of placing the magnificent sun in a new sunrise and sunset every day—with such precise calculation that it completes its annual cycle for millions of years without the slightest deficiency—is capable of bringing man back to life once more and granting him a new existence, or taking away one group and replacing them with a more worthy group.

Key Point: The Lord of "Sunrises" and "Sunsets"

In the verses of the Holy Quran, the expression "East" (sunrise) and "West" (sunset) sometimes appears in the singular form, such as in verse 115 of Al-Baqarah: وَلِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَ الْمَغْرِبُ ("To Allah belong the East and the West"). Sometimes it appears in the dual form, such as in verse 17 of Surah Ar-Rahman: رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَ رَبُّ الْمَغْرِبَيْنِ ("The Lord of the two Easts and the Lord of the two Wests"). And sometimes it appears in the plural form, Al-Mashariq and Al-Magharib, as in the verse under discussion.

Some short-sighted individuals have imagined these expressions to be contradictory, whereas they are all harmonious, and each refers to a specific point. To explain: the sun rises every day from a new point and sets in a new point; therefore, we have as many sunrises and sunsets as there are days in the year. On the other hand, among all these sunrises and sunsets, two sunrises and two sunsets are distinct: one at the beginning of summer (the maximum peak of the sun in the northern orbit) and one at the beginning of winter (the minimum descent of the sun in the southern orbit)—one is referred to as the Tropic of Cancer and the other as the Tropic of Capricorn. Since these two are completely distinct, they have been specifically emphasized. In addition to these, two other sunrises and sunsets, called the equinoctial sunrise and sunset (at the beginning of spring and the beginning of autumn when day and night are equal throughout the world), are also distinct, which some have taken رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَ رَبُّ الْمَغْرِبَيْنِ to refer to, and that is also noteworthy.

However, where it is mentioned in the singular form, it carries a generic meaning, where the focus is only on the principle of the East and the West without considering the individual points. Thus, each of the various expressions above contains a point and draws man's attention to the various changes in the rising and setting of the sun and the regular alteration of the solar orbits.

Ayahs

فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٤٢)
So leave them to gossip and play till they encounter the day they are promised: (42)
يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ سِرَاعًا كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ (٤٣)
the day when they emerge from graves, hastening, as if racing toward a target, (43)
خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (٤٤)
with a humbled look [in their eyes], overcast by abasement. That is the day they had been promised. (44)

Commentary

As If They Are Racing Toward Their Idols!

In these verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Ma'arij, as a warning and threat to the stubborn, mocking, and persistent disbelievers, He says: "So leave them to plunge in their vain talk and play until they meet their Day which they are promised" (فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَ يَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ) [^1]. No further reasoning or exhortation is necessary; they are neither people of logic nor do they possess the readiness to awaken. Let them be immersed in their falsehoods and nonsense, and like children occupied with play, until their promised day—the Day of Resurrection—arrives, and they see everything with their own eyes! This verse, with the exact same wording and without any difference, also appears in Surah az-Zukhruf, verse 83.

Then, He proceeds to introduce that promised day and describes signs of this terrifying and horrific day, saying: "The Day they will emerge from the graves rapidly as if they were racing toward their idols" (يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْداثِ سِراعاً كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلى‌ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ). What a strange description! It likens their state on the Day of Resurrection, as they move swiftly toward the Divine Court of Justice, to their rush toward their idols on a day of celebration or mourning. But how vast is the difference between the two? In reality, it is a mockery of the hollow beliefs they held in the life of this world.

"Ajdaath" (اجداث) is the plural of "jadath" (جدث), meaning "grave."

"Sira'an" (سراع) is the plural of "sari'" (سريع), meaning a person or thing that moves with speed.

[^1]: "Yakhudu" (يخوضوا) is derived from the root "khawd" (خوض), which originally means moving through water. It is then used metaphorically for instances where a person is immersed in false matters.

"Nusub" (نصب) is the plural of "nasib" (نصيب), and according to some, it is the plural of "nusb" (نصب). It originally means something that is fixed in a place. It was used to refer to idols that were fixed in certain locations as stone blocks, which they worshipped, sacrificed upon, and poured the blood of the sacrifice over. The difference between it and "sanam" (صنم) was that a "sanam" was an idol with a specific shape and form, whereas "nusub" were shapeless stone blocks worshipped for some reason. As we read in Surah al-Ma'idah, verse 3, among the forbidden meats is the animal slaughtered upon these idols (وَ ما ذُبِحَ عَلَى النُّصُبِ).

"Yufidun" (يوفضون) is from the root "ifadah" (افاضه), meaning rapid movement, similar to the swift flow of water from a spring.

Some have also said that "nusub" in the verse under discussion refers to flags or banners that armies or caravans set up at a point, and everyone rushes to reach them. However, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then, He mentions other signs, adding: "With their eyes humbled, looking down in terror" (خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ). "And a veil of humiliation and disgrace covers their entire being" (تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ) [^1]. At the end of the verse, He says: "That is the Day which they were promised" (ذلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ).

[^1]: "Tarhaquhum" (ترهقهم) is from the root "rahaq" (رهق), meaning to cover by force.

Yes, this is the very promised day that they used to mock. Sometimes they would say: "Even if such a day exists, our condition on that day will be better than that of the believers." But on that day, out of intense fear, horror, and shame, they will not dare to raise their heads. The dust of humiliation will have settled on their faces, and they will be engulfed in a halo of grief and sorrow. Certainly, on that day they will be regretful and remorseful, but what benefit will it bring?

O Allah! On that terrifying day, place us under the cover of Your mercy. O Lord! The traps of Satan are difficult, the whims of the soul are dominant, and long-term desires are deceptive; grant us awakening, awareness, and the grace not to deviate from the path.

O God! Place us among the believers who have fulfilled their covenant with You and have girded themselves for Your obedience. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Ma'arij 21 Dhu al-Hijjah al-Haram 1406
